description: The provided image has extremely low resolution and is identified as an irrelevant interface element, with only the text 'Check for updates' recognizable. No valid scientific content related to the research on low alloy ultrahigh strength steel toughened by high-density NiAl clusters can be extracted from this image, including no microstructure characterization results, mechanical property data, phase information, or material system related content matching the paper's research scope is available from the visual content.

This figure presents the mechanical properties of two low-alloy ultrahigh strength steels (0Al steel and 1.3Al steel) in water quenched (WQ) state and after tempering at 180 °C for 2 h. Subplot (a) displays engineering stress-strain curves, with four samples tested: 0Al, WQ; 0Al, 2h tempered; 1.3Al, WQ; 1.3Al, 2h tempered. In as-quenched state, 0Al steel shows an ultimate tensile strength (UTS) of ~2294 MPa and total elongation of ~9.5%, while the 1.3Al WQ sample has UTS of ~2145 MPa and lower elongation of ~4.3% that fractures before reaching peak strength. After tempering, both steels have UTS below 2000 MPa, while their total elongations increase to ~13.5% for the 0Al tempered sample and ~13.7% for the 1.3Al tempered sample. Subplot (b) shows Charpy U-notch impact absorbing energy (KU₂, unit J): 0Al WQ has KU₂ of 33 J, rising to 65 J after tempering; 1.3Al WQ has KU₂ of 23 J, which sharply increases to 80 J after tempering, a ~3.5-fold improvement. This figure supports the conclusion that 1.3Al steel with high-density NiAl clusters achieves excellent strength-toughness synergy after low-temperature tempering.

This figure contains two subgraphs for low alloy ultrahigh strength steel characterization. Subgraph (a) displays the microstructure of tempered 1.3Al steel with a 10 μm scale bar, accompanied by a high-magnification inset (0.2 μm scale bar) showing nanoscale structural features, and the steel presents a typical tempered martensitic structure. Subgraph (b) shows X-ray diffraction patterns of tempered 0Al and 1.3Al steels with 2θ ranging from 20° to 100°, where diffraction peaks correspond to α-Fe (martensite) and retained austenite (γ phase) for both samples. The calculated dislocation density is ~2.01×10^15 m⁻² for 0Al steel and ~2.20×10^15 m⁻² for 1.3Al steel, while retained austenite content is 1.0% and 1.8% respectively. These results confirm that Al addition slightly increases dislocation density and retained austenite content, supporting that Al alloying modulates steel microstructure to achieve improved toughness.

This set of transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images characterize the microstructures of 0Al steel and 1.3Al low-alloy ultrahigh strength steel after 970 °C solution treatment, water quenching, and 180 °C tempering for 2 h. Subfigures (a, d) are bright-field TEM: (a) shows lath martensite with sharp lath boundaries in 0Al steel, (d) shows martensite with higher density of dislocations inside laths in 1.3Al steel. Subfigures (b, e) are dark-field TEM with corresponding selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns, identifying thin-film retained austenite distributed at lath boundaries, with higher content in 1.3Al steel. Subfigures (c, f) are high-magnification TEM with SAED patterns, verifying needle-like ε-carbides precipitated within martensite laths in both steels. The observed microstructure differences support that Al addition induced high-density NiAl clusters regulate microstructure to achieve excellent strength-toughness synergy.

This figure displays the multilevel martensitic structures of two experimental low alloy ultrahigh strength steels, namely 0Al steel and 1.3Al steel, characterized via EBSD, SEM, and TEM. Subfigure (a) illustrates prior austenite grains with a 20μm scale bar, (b) shows martensitic packets with a 10μm scale bar, (c) presents martensitic blocks with a 5μm scale bar, and (d) demonstrates martensitic laths with a 0.2μm scale bar. Quantitative measurements confirm that the average prior austenite grain size is 26.1±9.4μm for 0Al steel and 23.4±5.9μm for 1.3Al steel, with comparable sizes of martensitic packets, blocks, and laths between the two samples. These results indicate that martensitic unit size is not the primary factor governing the toughness difference between the two steels, which is instead dominated by differences in precipitation behavior.

This figure presents multiscale characterization of precipitates in 1.3Al low alloy ultrahigh strength steel after 970 °C solution treatment, water quenching, and 180 °C tempering for 2 h. Subfigures (a) and (d) are atom probe tomography isoconcentration surfaces: (a) 5 at.% C maps show uniformly distributed needle-like ε-carbides with ~2.2 nm thickness and ~10 nm spacing, while (d) 10 at.% Ni+Al maps show near-spherical NiAl clusters with number density of 4.88×10²⁴ m⁻³ and equivalent radius of 0.57 nm. Subfigures (b) and (e) are high-resolution TEM images with fast Fourier transform patterns: (b) confirms ε-carbides have hcp structure along [110]ε zone axis in bcc martensite matrix, (e) identifies NiAl clusters as B2-ordered bcc structure along [001] zone axis, fully coherent with the matrix. Subfigures (c) and (f) are proximity histograms: (c) shows ε-carbides are C-rich (up to ~9 at.% C) with slight Ni/Al enrichment, (f) shows NiAl clusters have ~13 at.% Ni and Al, which is below stoichiometric NiAl indicating early nucleation stage, with slight Si/Mo/Cr segregation. This figure verifies that ε-carbides and NiAl clusters nucleate and grow independently during tempering with no competitive elemental partitioning between C and Ni/Al, supporting the dual-precipitation toughening mechanism for the studied steel.

This figure presents atom probe tomography (APT) characterization results of 1.3Al low alloy ultrahigh strength steel, which was processed by 970 °C solution treatment for 1 h followed by water quenching, then tempering at 180 °C for 2 h and water quenching. The central 3D reconstructed atom map with 20 nm scale bar marks 10 at.% NiAl isoconcentration surfaces (green) and 5 at.% C isoconcentration surfaces (brown). Subfigure A shows Ni, Al, C atomic distribution across an ε-carbide cross-section. Subfigure B is a proximity histogram for dispersed NiAl clusters, showing Ni and Al concentrations rise inside clusters while C concentration remains ~1 at.% around clusters. Subfigure C displays 4 at.% Cr+Mo, 7 at.% Si isoconcentration surfaces and a proximity histogram for segregated NiAl clusters, showing Si, Cr, Mo enrich around NiAl clusters. The results confirm ε-carbides and NiAl clusters nucleate and grow independently during tempering without competitive partitioning between C and Ni/Al, and Si-enriched regions provide favorable nucleation sites for NiAl clusters.

This figure presents load-deflection curves from Charpy impact tests for two low-alloy ultrahigh strength steels, i.e., 0Al steel (without high-density NiAl clusters) and 1.3Al steel (with high-density coherent NiAl clusters). Subplot (a) compares the two load-deflection curves, marking yield load (P_GY, square symbol) and maximum load (P_M, circle symbol) for each sample. Subplots (b) and (c) show the integrated load-energy-deflection curves for 0Al and 1.3Al steel, respectively. The 0Al steel has a peak load P_M of 39.7 kN, crack initiation energy E_i of 45.0 J, crack propagation energy E_p of 17.4 J, and total impact energy KU₂ of 62.4 J, with peak load reached at 2.06 mm deflection. The 1.3Al steel shows a peak load P_M of 38.6 kN, E_i of 62.0 J (37.8% higher than 0Al), E_p of 21.0 J (20.7% higher than 0Al), total KU₂ of 83.0 J, with peak load achieved at 2.55 mm deflection. Both steels show rapid load drop after peak load without obvious crack arrest stage. The results demonstrate that the introduction of high-density NiAl clusters significantly improves both crack initiation and propagation resistance, leading to superior impact toughness of the 1.3Al steel, supporting the core conclusion that NiAl clusters enable excellent strength-toughness synergy in this steel system.

This figure presents impact fracture morphologies of two low alloy ultrahigh strength steels. Subfigure (a) shows the macroscopic fracture surface of 0Al steel with a 1 mm scale bar, while subfigures (b) (100 μm scale) and (c) (25 μm scale) display microscopic features of the marked region in (a), featuring quasi-cleavage fracture and limited dimples. Subfigure (d) shows the macroscopic fracture surface of 1.3Al steel (with high-density coherent NiAl clusters) with a 1 mm scale bar, while subfigures (e) (100 μm scale) and (f) (25 μm scale) show its microscopic fracture features. Compared to 0Al steel, 1.3Al steel exhibits significantly increased number and size of primary dimples, more prominent tearing ridges, higher surface roughness, and dominant ductile fracture via dimple growth and coalescence, which indicates higher impact toughness. This fractographic evidence confirms that high-density NiAl clusters improve the toughness of the ultrahigh strength steel, supporting the reported strength-toughness synergy of the developed steel.

This figure presents electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD) characterizations of cross-sectional features near impact fracture main cracks for 0Al and 1.3Al low alloy ultrahigh strength steels, with all subfigures having a scale bar of 10 μm. Subfigure (a) shows the band contrast + inverse pole figure + grain boundary (BC+IPF+GB) map for 0Al steel, and (b) is the corresponding kernel average misorientation (KAM) map reflecting local strain distribution, with an average KAM value of 1.16°, exhibiting inhomogeneous strain distribution as high-strain regions concentrate around the crack while large undeformed low-strain regions exist in the adjacent matrix. Subfigure (c) shows the BC+IPF+GB map for 1.3Al steel, and (d) is its corresponding KAM map with an average KAM value of 1.31°, showing much more uniform strain distribution across both the fracture surface and surrounding matrix. The results confirm that high-density NiAl clusters in 1.3Al steel promote uniform dislocation structure formation, mitigate stress concentration near cracks, and contribute to improved fracture toughness.

This set of scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images presents the vertical-section fracture microstructures of fracture toughness tested low alloy ultrahigh strength steel specimens. Subfigure (a) (50 μm scale bar) shows low-magnification morphology of 0Al steel, with (c) (10 μm scale bar) as the magnified view of the blue box in (a); subfigure (b) (50 μm scale bar) shows low-magnification morphology of 1.3Al steel containing high-density NiAl clusters, with (d) (10 μm scale bar) as the magnified view of the blue box in (b). The 0Al steel exhibits a relatively straight main crack path, few micro-voids, occasional crack deflection followed by rapid tip passivation, and negligible secondary cracking. The 1.3Al steel shows frequent main crack deflections, abundant 5–10 μm micro-voids and secondary cracks 8–18 μm below the main crack, with void coalescence forming secondary cracks. These features increase crack propagation distance and energy dissipation, confirming the toughening effect induced by high-density NiAl clusters in 1.3Al steel.
